Final Project - Predicting PDAC with Circulating miRNA

2026-04-19

Preparing Data

Import Data

We use the normalized miRNA count matrix from GEO, apply a single log2 transform with a pseudocount of 1, and then merge it with the demographic metadata. Categorical variables are set to factors and the PDAC case and control variable is set to binary for modeling.

That imported log-transformed miRNA matrix is then used throughout the analysis, including blocked limma and all downstream predictive modeling.

The side-by-side QQ plots below show how the global miRNA value distribution shifts after the import-time log2 transform.

The dataset is fully observed with no missing values, so we can proceed without imputation.

Create Training and Holdout Sets

To split the data while respecting matched-pairs, we performed the split on the matched set IDs, ensuring each pair is not split between sets.

Training and holdout set sizes
dataset rows
Training 2090
Holdout 524
Training and holdout class balance
dataset class n_samples
Training Controls 1045
Training PDAC case 1045
Holdout Controls 262
Holdout PDAC case 262

Perform Differential Expression Filtering

The limma filtering is based on a blocked linear model with duplicate-correlation adjustment for matched sets and is run once on the full training set. Because the miRNA columns are log2-transformed during import, blocked limma and the downstream predictive models operate on the same log-expression scale. In that model, CC is the term of interest and Sex, Race, and Age are adjustment covariates. The top N miRNAs are selected based on the smallest limma p-values, with ties broken by the absolute moderated t-statistic. To avoid rerunning the expensive blocked-limma fit on every render, the report loads the saved full-training limma object by default; set run_limma_analysis <- TRUE once after changing upstream preprocessing if you need to rebuild Data/Full train_filter_results.rds.

The top-ranked miRNAs table has 20 rows and 10 columns. The selected miRNAs vector has 20 miRNAs. These top 20 miRNAs are then used as the shared candidate feature set for all models in the subsequent cross-validation step, with the rationale that this filtering step is necessary to reduce the feature space to a level that can be effectively handled by the models given the sample size, while still being broad enough to allow for model-specific feature selection and not biasing towards any one model’s selection criteria.

Cross-Validation Loop

We perform a 5-fold cross-validation on the training set of paired samples, ensuring matched-samples are kept together in the same fold.

Within each fold: 1. We restrict the log2-transformed normalized count matrix to the top 20 miRNAs selected from the limma analysis on the full training set. 2. We then fit each model using that shared subset of selected miRNAs together with Sex, Race, and Age in the design.

Cross-validation AUC and accuracy by model
model n_predictions cv_auc cv_accuracy
enet 2090 0.568 0.547
lda 2090 0.566 0.545
lasso 2090 0.566 0.549
forward_step 2090 0.564 0.537
random_forest 2090 0.564 0.544
decision_tree 2090 0.515 0.516

Holdout Evaluation

Evaluate the final fold, using the calibrated models from the CV step, on the holdout set. We report AUC and accuracy for each model on the holdout set, as well as the number of features selected in each final model and the number of miRNAs that passed the initial limma filter.

Downstream multiMiR analysis uses all 8 miRNAs in this top-ranked consensus set.

Multiple miRNAs tied for the highest model-selection count, so the table below is ordered by the smallest blocked-limma p-value, with ties broken by the absolute moderated t-statistic and then miRNA identifier. All rows in this table are used for downstream multiMiR annotation.

Multiple variables tied for the highest model-selection count, so miRNA variables in the table below are ordered by the smallest blocked-limma p-value, with ties broken by the absolute moderated t-statistic and then variable identifier. Demographic covariates do not have blocked-limma statistics and are listed after miRNA variables. The first row is the top-ranked consensus variable used for downstream annotation.

miRNA Interactions

Assess validated target and disease/drug associations for the top-ranked consensus miRNA set with multiMiR.

multiMiR query miRNAs: hsa-miR-374c-5p, hsa-miR-3605-3p, hsa-miR-4647, hsa-let-7b-5p, hsa-miR-3130-3p, hsa-miR-643, hsa-miR-320d, and hsa-miR-600

Validated multiMiR interaction records across the top-ranked consensus miRNA set: 17460; unique target genes: 11633; queried miRNAs with validated targets: 8 of 8
